stdout:
OBSERVATIONS:
- Category 2 (texture): Image 1's mottling is isotropic blob noise — blotches are roughly uniform in size and randomly scattered with no directional bias, resembling a Perlin/reaction-diffusion pattern. Image 2's texture is clearly directional, with elongated streaks running roughly vertically/diagonally, consistent with the rolling process of real sheet glass.
- Category 3 (color/translucency): Image 1's color transitions between light and dark blotches are uniformly soft-edged and low-frequency throughout the whole frame — every blotch has nearly identical edge softness. Image 2 shows a mix of sharp micro-transitions (small pinpoint bright flecks with crisp edges, e.g. near center and lower-right) alongside broader soft color washes, i.e. two different length scales of variation as you'd expect from texture-driven light scattering.
- Category 1 (specularity): Image 1 has no discrete specular highlight anywhere in the frame — brightness only varies as a smooth low-frequency field. Image 2 has multiple small bright pinpoint glints (e.g. the sharp white dot left-of-center and near right edge) that look like actual reflected highlights catching surface bumps.
- Category 5 (lighting): Image 1's brightness falls off smoothly from top to bottom in a way that is decoupled from the local blob texture — the blobs keep constant contrast against each other as the whole field darkens, as if a separate vignette/gradient layer was multiplied over a texture. Image 2's brightness variation tracks the texture itself (streaks get brighter/darker together), consistent with backlight passing through variable local thickness.
- Category 6 (camera optics): Image 1 shows no visible sensor grain even in its darkest lower region, and all texture edges are soft/blurred with no crisp micro-detail anywhere. Image 2 has crisp fine detail and small sharp specks (possible bubbles/dust) coexisting with soft regions, more typical of real optics resolving actual surface relief.

VERDICT: Image 1 synthetic -- its blotch texture and brightness gradient behave like independent generic noise/vignette layers rather than a physically coupled light-through-texture interaction, and it lacks any specular highlight or camera grain.
